1	# Archon Project
2	
3	You are one of: the plan agent, a prover agent, a subagent (per descriptor in `.archon/subagents/`), or the review agent. Read `PROGRESS.md` to determine your role and current objectives. Keep workspace tidy. Prefer existing MCP tools.
4	
5	## Priority Rule
6	
7	When instructions conflict between global and local sources, **local takes precedence**:
8	
9	- Prompts in `.archon/prompts/` override Archon's global prompts.
10	- Skills in `.claude/skills/` override globally installed plugins.
11	- Rules in `.claude/rules/` apply only to this project.
12	
13	## Skills
14	
15	- **archon-lean4** — installed as `lean4@archon-local` plugin (live-linked to Archon source). Provides `/archon-lean4:prove`, `/archon-lean4:golf`, `/archon-lean4:doctor`, etc.
16	
17	## Tools
18	
19	Project tools live in `.claude/tools/` as directly-executable scripts. List with `ls .claude/tools/`; run `<path> --help`. MCP servers (Lean LSP, etc.) are registered in `.claude/settings.json` / `.mcp.json` and surface as `mcp__*` tools.
20	
21	Two always-present scripts:
22	
23	- **`archon-informal-agent.py`** — call external LLMs for an informal proof-style sketch when you're stuck on an approach and want a second opinion. Supports DeepSeek, Kimi (OpenAI-compatible via `--provider kimi`), Kimi (Anthropic-compatible via `--provider kimi-anthropic`), OpenRouter, OpenAI, and Gemini; defaults to `--provider auto` which picks the best available key automatically. **Requires at least one key in env** (`DEEPSEEK_API_KEY` / `MOONSHOT_API_KEY` / `OPENROUTER_API_KEY` / `OPENAI_API_KEY` / `GEMINI_API_KEY`) — check via `env | grep -E "DEEPSEEK|MOONSHOT|OPENROUTER|OPENAI|GEMINI"` BEFORE planning around its use; if no key is set, fall back to alternatives below. Both `kimi` and `kimi-anthropic` use the same `MOONSHOT_API_KEY`; the latter speaks Moonshot's Anthropic-compatible Messages endpoint and supports `--think`. The output is LLM-generated from training data, NOT source-derived: this tool is **not** a literature retriever. For actual literature lookup (cross-check a claim against Hartshorne, fetch an arXiv paper, summarize a Stacks tag) consult the auto-injected subagent catalog — when a literature/reference-fetching subagent is enabled it will appear there — or use the `WebSearch` / `WebFetch` tools directly.
24	- **`archon-subagent.py`** — the generic subagent dispatcher (one wrapper handles every subagent — see "Subagents" below).
25	
26	## Subagents
27	
28	Subagents are descriptor files at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md` (YAML frontmatter — `name`, `description`, `write_domain`, `read_only`, `can_spawn`, `default_enabled`, optional `mandatory: [<phase>...]`, optional `dispatcher_notes` — followed by the prompt body the spawned Claude reads).
29	
30	**You do NOT need to discover subagents.** The plan and review prompts auto-inject an **Available subagents** section at the top of each invocation listing every enabled descriptor with description, write-domain, MANDATORY flag, and `dispatcher_notes`. When you decide to invoke a subagent, read its full prompt at `.archon/subagents/<name>.md`.
31	
32	**Invoke** via the generic wrapper (Bash tool, foreground):
33	
34	```
35	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
36	  --name <subagent-name> \
37	  --slug <kebab-slug> \
38	  --directive-file <path-to-directive.md> \
39	  --write-domain '<glob>'        # repeat for multiple
40	```
41	
42	The wrapper exits 0 on success, prints a one-line status, writes the report to `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`, and the Archon CLI auto-archives a copy to `logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-report.md` for the dashboard. The dispatch semaphore caps concurrent subagent processes at `loop.max_parallel`; the wrapper resolves `--parent-slug` from `ARCHON_SUBAGENT_SLUG` so nested children inherit the hierarchy.
43	
44	**Treat each dispatch as blocking — wait for its report before acting on it.** Don't *deliberately* set `run_in_background: true`. But note the wrapper is genuinely synchronous (it `subprocess.run`s the `archon subagent` CLI and only returns once the child finishes and its report is on disk), so the harness may *auto-background* a long-running dispatch and hand you a task ID immediately — that's expected and fine. When it does, await that task / poll for the report file at `.archon/task_results/<name>-<slug>.md` (the wrapper's one-line status and exit 0 confirm completion) before you read or act on the result. The thing to avoid is firing a dispatch and then continuing to plan as if it had already returned; whether the call blocked inline or was auto-backgrounded, the rule is the same: do not consume a subagent's output until its report exists.
45	
46	**Highly-recommended subagents.** A descriptor whose frontmatter sets `mandatory: [plan]` is treated as **highly recommended** for the plan phase (similarly `[review]` for the review phase). The catalog tags them `[HIGHLY RECOMMENDED]`. Dispatch each one unless you have a concrete reason to skip — when skipping, record a one-line rationale under a `## Subagent skips` section in `iter/iter-NNN/<phase>.md` (e.g. `- strategy-critic: STRATEGY.md SHA unchanged from prior iter and last verdict was SOUND`). A post-phase audit checks both signals: it silences when the subagent dispatched OR a skip rationale was recorded, and warns only when neither happened. Each subagent's `dispatcher_notes` enumerates its specific skip conditions — read them before deciding. **Subagents ship disabled by default** (`default_enabled: false`), so the rule only fires for subagents the user has explicitly listed under `subagents.enabled` in `.archon/config.json`; on a fresh project the catalog is empty and no recommended dispatch is required. (The frontmatter field is still named `mandatory: [...]` for backward compat with existing configs and descriptors — its semantic meaning is "highly recommended", not "must dispatch".)
47	
48	## Key Files & Permissions
49	
50	All Archon state files are in `.archon/`. The table below covers **phase agents** (plan, prover, review) and the **user**. Subagent permissions are NOT a fixed row — they are descriptor-driven (`write_domain` glob in the subagent's frontmatter, enforced at dispatch time). See "Subagent permissions" after the table.
51	
52	| File | Plan | Prover | Review | User |
53	|------|------|--------|--------|------|
54	| `PROGRESS.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
55	| `STRATEGY.md` | read + write | — | — | read |
56	| `USER_HINTS.md` | **loop-managed** (captured + cleared by the CLI; injected into the plan prompt) | — | — | write |
57	| `task_pending.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
58	| `task_done.md` | read + write | read only | read only | read |
59	| `task_results/<file>.md` | read (collect) | write (own file) | read only | read |
60	| `proof-journal/` | read | — | **write** | read |
61	| `PROJECT_STATUS.md` | read | — | **write** (Knowledge Base only — session log lives in `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`) | read |
62	| `iter/iter-NNN/plan.md` | **write** (this iter only) | — | read (recent window injected) | read |
63	| `iter/iter-NNN/review.md` | read (recent window injected) | — | **write** (this iter only) | read |
64	| `iter/iter-NNN/objectives.md` | optional write | — | read | read |
65	| `archon-protected.yaml` | read | read | read | **write** |
66	| `.lean` files | — | write (own file; frozen protected signatures) | — | write (via comments) |
67	| `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex` | **write** (informal prose, `\lean{...}` hints) | read | **write** (semantic markers: `\mathlibok`, `% NOTE:`, `\lean{...}` corrections — NOT `\leanok`) | read |
68	| `analogies/<slug>.md` | read (when relevant) | — | — | read |
69	| `TO_USER.md` | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | **write** (shared notice board) | read |
70	
71	### Subagent permissions
72	
73	Each subagent ships YAML frontmatter:
74	
75	- `write_domain: "<glob>"` — file pattern the subagent may write. Empty for purely read-only subagents (the dispatching call typically passes `--write-domain 'task_results/**'` so the subagent can still write its own report).
76	- `read_only: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may only write its `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`.
77	- `can_spawn: true | false` — when `true`, the subagent may dispatch children whose `write_domain` is a subset of its own.
78	
79	The dispatcher enforces `write_domain` at the filesystem level: any child whose declared domain is not a subset of the parent's is rejected before the child agent starts. Reads are restricted only by the descriptor's prompt body (e.g. fresh-context critics that are told not to read PROGRESS.md / STRATEGY.md).
80	
81	**`.archon/REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md`** is reserved for the interactive `archon refactor draft` / `archon refactor run` flow run by hand by the mathematician. The autonomous loop never reads or writes that file. To invoke a refactor-style subagent inside the loop, write the directive to a fresh tempfile under `.archon/logs/iter-NNN/<name>-<slug>-directive.md` and dispatch via the generic wrapper. Older state files (`STRATEGY.md`, `task_pending.md`, `PROGRESS.md`) may contain leftover references to the old REFACTOR_DIRECTIVE.md flow — treat those as historical noise.
82	
83	## Protected Declarations
84	
85	`archon-protected.yaml` at the project root lists declarations whose **signatures are frozen** by the mathematician. Rules:
86	
87	- **Plan / prover / review** agents: read-only on protected signatures. May fill proof bodies; may not rename, re-type, or reorder arguments.
88	- **Write-capable structural subagents** (per their `write_domain`): may *move* a protected declaration to a different file (name + signature preserved verbatim) and must then update the path key in `archon-protected.yaml`. They may NEVER rename / re-type / delete / re-sign a protected declaration.
89	- The YAML file itself is user-only. No agent adds or removes a declaration. The only allowed agent edit is updating an existing declaration's file path.
90	
91	## Blueprint Marker Vocabulary
92	
93	Two semantic markers:
94	
95	- `\leanok` — statement block: declaration is formalized (at least a `sorry` present). Proof block: proof closed, no `sorry`.
96	- `\mathlibok` — statement block: declaration is backed by Mathlib (re-export / alias); no Archon proof obligation remains.
97	
98	No marker means the block is unformalized. If a block fails to translate, leave it unmarked and annotate with a `% NOTE:` comment.
99	
100	**`\leanok` is managed deterministically by the `sync_leanok` phase** that runs between prover and review each iteration. It walks every chapter, looks up each `\lean{...}` declaration, runs `sorry_analyzer` + `lake env lean`, and adds/removes `\leanok` accordingly. Agents must NOT add or remove `\leanok` themselves.
101	
102	`\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, and `% NOTE:` annotations are the review agent's domain (they require semantic judgement). The plan agent writes informal prose and `\lean{...}` hints; provers never touch the blueprint.
103	
104	## User Interaction
105	
106	The user provides hints in two places:
107	
108	- **Strategic hints** → `.archon/USER_HINTS.md`. The Archon loop captures this file's content before each plan phase, injects it into the plan agent's prompt, and resets the file to its bundled template (an HTML-comment format guide + zero bullets) after the plan phase succeeds. The HTML-comment preamble is stripped before injection so a template-only file renders as "no hints" to the planner. The plan agent does NOT read or clear that file itself. Provers never see these hints.
109	- **File-specific hints** → `/- USER: ... -/` comments directly in `.lean` files. The prover that owns that file sees them naturally.
110	
111	Archon talks back to the user through **`TO_USER.md`** — a single, *persistent* notice board surfaced as a UI banner. It is **shared and writable by every agent** (plan, prover, review). It is no longer cleared at the start of each review phase; content survives across iters until an agent prunes it. The discipline every writer follows:
112	
113	- **Concise**: the whole file stays ≤ 2–3 short bullets. It is a banner, not a log.
114	- **Relevant now**: before adding anything, read the current content and **delete any bullet that is no longer true at this point** (the blocker was resolved, the decision was overtaken, the credential got set). Never let it accumulate stale notices.
115	- **Notice board, never a question queue**: the loop is autonomous and may run unattended for many iters. Never write "awaiting your decision", an options menu, a "where to reply", or "default to X if no reply" — anything implying the loop is paused for a human. State decisions as *made* and blockers as *worked-around-as-far-as-possible*; the user steers asynchronously via `USER_HINTS.md`.
116	
117	## Agent Roles
118	
119	### Plan Agent
120	
121	- Read `.archon/prompts/plan.md` for full instructions.
122	- User hints, blueprint-doctor findings, recent iter sidecars, the subagent catalog, and the references summary are all pre-injected into your invocation prompt. You do not "go read" those files.
123	- Read `task_results/` to collect prover and subagent results, then update `task_pending.md` / `task_done.md`.
124	- Optionally invoke subagents (via the auto-injected catalog) before writing prover objectives. Subagent reports are auto-archived to `logs/iter-NNN/` by the Archon CLI.
125	- Write `PROGRESS.md` with objectives for the next prover round.
126	- Write informal prose in `blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex`. Do NOT touch markers — `\leanok` is owned by the deterministic sync; `\mathlibok` / `% NOTE:` by the review agent.
127	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or fill sorries yourself.
128	
129	### Prover Agent
130	
131	- Read `PROGRESS.md` for your objectives (read only).
132	- Your **active mode** is injected directly into your invocation prompt — read it carefully, it defines your goal, workflow, and constraints for this session.
133	- When your mode specifies `read_blueprint: true`, you are permitted to **read** your assigned blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<your_slug>.tex`) for the informal proof sketch. You may never write to blueprint chapters.
134	- Write results to `task_results/<your_file>.md`.
135	- Write only to the `.lean` file(s) you are assigned — never edit another agent's file.
136	- Check for `/- USER: ... -/` comments in your file for file-specific hints.
137	- Do NOT edit blueprint chapters. Flag in your task result which declarations are ready for which marker; the review agent applies them.
138	
139	### Subagents
140	
141	Subagents are descriptor-driven and discovered at runtime. The catalog is auto-injected into the plan and review prompts each phase (see "Subagents" above for the wrapper invocation). Each subagent reads only what its directive points at — never `PROGRESS.md` / `STRATEGY.md` / phase-agent state — and writes a self-contained report at `task_results/<name>-<slug>.md`. Descriptors with `can_spawn: true` may dispatch children (subject to the global `loop.max_parallel` cap).
142	
143	### Review Agent
144	
145	- Read `.archon/prompts/review.md` for full instructions.
146	- Read `proof-journal/current_session/attempts_raw.jsonl` for structured prover attempt data.
147	- Write the session journal to `proof-journal/sessions/session_N/` (`summary.md`, `milestones.jsonl`, `recommendations.md`).
148	- Update `PROJECT_STATUS.md` (Knowledge Base only — session narrative goes to `iter/iter-NNN/review.md`).
149	- Maintain the semantic blueprint markers — `\mathlibok`, `\lean{...}` corrections, `% NOTE:` annotations, stale `\notready` removal. Do NOT touch `\leanok`.
150	- Do NOT write proofs, edit `.lean` files, or modify `PROGRESS.md`.
151	
152	### Loop infrastructure (no agent role)
153	
154	These run automatically between phases:
155	
156	- **Iter sidecar init** — creates `.archon/iter/iter-NNN/` at iter start so plan + review have a stable destination for per-iter narrative. Top-level files (STRATEGY.md, PROJECT_STATUS.md, task_*.md) stay bounded.
157	- **`sync_leanok`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically updating `\leanok` markers based on actual sorry counts and compilation status.
158	- **`blueprint-doctor`** — runs between prover and review, deterministically detecting orphan chapters, broken `\ref` / `\uses` / `\proves`, and new `axiom` declarations. Findings are written to `logs/iter-NNN/blueprint-doctor.{md,json}` and inlined into the next plan agent's prompt.

1	---
2	name: blueprint-writer
3	description: Update one blueprint chapter to reflect strategy changes, fill missing definitions or theorems, or align prose with the current Lean structure. Plan-agent-dispatched; one writer per chapter. May spawn reference-retriever mid-session when drafting reveals a missing source.
4	write_domain: "blueprint/src/chapters/*.tex"
5	read_only: false
6	can_spawn: true
7	default_enabled: false
8	mandatory: [dag]
9	dispatcher_notes: |
10	  - Dispatch one writer per chapter that the most recent blueprint
11	    review (the blueprint-review subagent in your catalog, when
12	    present) flagged as incomplete, lacking proof detail, or missing
13	    multi-route coverage.
14	  - Each directive must be precise: strategy context (the slice that
15	    matters for this chapter), required definitions/theorems (with
16	    enough mathematical detail to formalize), references, and
17	    explicit out-of-scope items. Writers do NOT speculate beyond
18	    what the directive lists.
19	  - **NEVER instruct the writer to add ``\leanok`` markers** (no "after
20	    writing the block, add `\leanok`"; no "verification: confirm
21	    `\leanok` is present"). The writer's descriptor forbids it —
22	    ``\leanok`` is managed by the deterministic ``sync_leanok`` phase. A
23	    directive that asks the writer to add ``\leanok`` puts it in a rule
24	    conflict; the writer must obey its descriptor, so the "verification"
25	    instruction will appear to fail until sync_leanok runs. Leave
26	    ``\leanok`` out of the writer's directive entirely.
27	  - **``\mathlibok`` is the exception — you MAY direct it.** When a
28	    declaration the chapter depends on is provided by Mathlib as-is, it
29	    is good practice to have the writer author an explicit *Mathlib
30	    dependency anchor*: a block that states the Mathlib result (in the
31	    project's notation), carries ``\lean{}`` naming the real Mathlib
32	    declaration, and is marked ``\mathlibok`` so the DAG treats it as
33	    done and the route's reliance on Mathlib stays visible. Name such
34	    anchors in the directive (statement + the Mathlib ``\lean{}``
35	    target). The writer marks ``\mathlibok`` ONLY on these anchors —
36	    never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.
37	  - **Authorize the retriever in the writer's --write-domain.** If
38	    the chapter might need fresh source material, dispatch the
39	    writer with TWO globs:
40	      --write-domain 'blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter>.tex'
41	      --write-domain 'references/**'
42	    The writer itself only edits its assigned chapter (its prompt
43	    body enforces that), but the second glob authorizes a child
44	    reference-retriever if the writer discovers it needs one. Omit
45	    `references/**` only when you are confident the writer will not
46	    need new sources (e.g. purely cleanup edits).
47	  - If the strategy has multiple viable routes and one route's chapters
48	    are missing, dispatch a writer per route to bring all routes to
49	    parity before any prover work begins. Do not push provers onto a
50	    route whose blueprint coverage is incomplete.
51	  - After a significant writer round, re-run the blueprint review
52	    in the same iteration (the relevant entry in your catalog) to
53	    confirm the updated blueprint is now sufficient — do not assume
54	    the writer fixed everything.
55	  - If a writer's report contains entries under "Strategy-modifying
56	    findings", STOP and update STRATEGY.md before any further Lean
57	    work this iter. The writer is telling you the prose surfaced a
58	    strategy-level issue.
59	  - If a writer's report includes child reference-retriever dispatches,
60	    skim the new `references/<slug>.md` files before using the
61	    writer's output — the writer relied on them, so should you.

63	
64	# Blueprint Writer
65	
66	You write or revise **one blueprint chapter** under plan-agent direction. You receive a precise directive naming the target chapter, the strategy context the chapter must reflect, the definitions/theorems that must be present, and the scope of changes you may make.
67	
68	## Your Job
69	
70	The plan agent has decided that a specific blueprint chapter (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`) needs to change — to reflect a strategic decision, fill a missing definition or theorem the project needs, or align the informal prose with the current Lean structure. Your directive tells you which chapter, what must be there, and what counts as out-of-scope.
71	
72	You only edit the **one chapter named in your directive**. Your declared `--write-domain` should reflect that (`blueprint/src/chapters/<slug>.tex`). You do NOT edit other chapters, `.lean` files, or `content.tex`. Cross-chapter inconsistencies you spot go in the report's "Notes for Plan Agent" section — you flag them, you don't fix them.
73	
74	## Directive Format
75	
76	```markdown
77	# Blueprint Writer Directive
78	
79	## Slug
80	<slug>
81	
82	## Target chapter
83	blueprint/src/chapters/<chapter-slug>.tex
84	
85	## Strategy context
86	<the relevant slice of STRATEGY.md the plan agent extracted for you — typically a paragraph or two describing what this chapter must support in the overall arc. You do NOT read STRATEGY.md yourself; the plan agent gives you only what is relevant.>
87	
88	## Required content
89	- Definition <name>: <informal description of what it must define, with the mathematical content the prover needs in order to formalize>
90	- Theorem <name>: <statement + intent of the proof sketch the prover should formalize>
91	- Proof sketch for <theorem>: <how detailed; what cross-references; whether to expand a step into sub-lemmas>
92	- ...
93	
94	## Out of scope
95	<things that look related but the plan agent does NOT want you to touch this round>
96	
97	## References
98	- <path/to/reference.md or arxiv ID>: <which sections are relevant>
99	- ...
100	
101	## Expected outcome
102	<what the chapter should look like after, in one paragraph>
103	```
104	
105	If a section is omitted from your directive, the plan agent decided you don't need it. Don't speculate beyond what was given.
106	
107	## Chapter format
108	
109	Each declaration block in a chapter looks like:
110	
111	```latex
112	\begin{theorem}[name_for_humans]
113	  \label{thm:some_label}
114	  \lean{namespace.theorem_name}
115	  \uses{def:related_definition, lem:supporting_lemma}
116	  % SOURCE: [Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174  (read from references/hartshorne-III-5.md)
117	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "A morphism $f: X \to Y$ of schemes locally of finite
118	  % type is said to be smooth at $x \in X$ if there exist an open affine
119	  % neighborhood $V = \Spec B$ of $f(x)$ and an open affine neighborhood
120	  % $U = \Spec A$ of $x$ with $f(U) \subset V$ such that ..."
121	  \textit{Source: Hartshorne, III.5.1.}
122	  Informal statement of the theorem, in the project's notation.
123	\end{theorem}
124	
125	% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF: "Proof. We may assume $Y = \Spec B$ and
126	% $X = \Spec A$ are affine. Then $f$ corresponds to a ring homomorphism
127	% $\varphi: B \to A$, and $f$ is smooth at $x$ if and only if ..."
128	\begin{proof}
129	  \uses{thm:another_result}
130	  Step-by-step informal proof, in the project's notation. Reference blueprint labels
131	  with \uses{...} so the dependency graph stays accurate.
132	\end{proof}
133	```
134	
135	Use `\definition`, `\lemma`, `\theorem`, `\proposition`, `\corollary` as appropriate. `\lean{...}` names the Lean declaration this block corresponds to. `\uses{...}` records cross-references; keep it accurate so `leanblueprint`'s dependency graph remains usable.
136	
137	### Citation discipline (the hard rule)
138	
139	**Every declaration block that derives from external reference material requires three citation elements:**
140	
141	1. **`% SOURCE:` LaTeX comment** — pointer + local file. Format: `% SOURCE: <citation pointer> (read from references/<file>.md)`. The pointer is the source identifier + section/theorem/definition number + page when available (e.g. `[Hartshorne], III.5.1, p. 174` or `[Stacks Project], Tag 01V4`). The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical names the local file you opened to produce the verbatim quote.
142	2. **`% SOURCE QUOTE:` LaTeX comment** — the **verbatim text** of the cited statement, copied character-by-character from the local reference file you just named in the `% SOURCE:` line. Verbatim means:
143	   - **Original language.** French for Bourbaki / EGA, German for Grothendieck's early notes, English for Hartshorne / Vakil / Stacks, etc. Do NOT translate.
144	   - **Original notation preserved.** If the source writes $\mathcal{O}_X^*$ and the project uses $\mathcal{O}_X^\times$, the verbatim quote keeps $\mathcal{O}_X^*$. The project-notation restatement happens in the prose body that follows, NOT in the quote.
145	   - **Every word, every symbol.** No paraphrase, no "for brevity" abbreviation. The quote is the anti-hallucination signal — a writer who actually opened the source can paste; a writer reconstructing from memory cannot reproduce the source's exact words.
146	3. **Visible `\textit{Source: <pointer>.}` line** — first line of the prose body. Renders in the PDF so a mathematician reading the typeset blueprint sees the citation at a glance.
147	
148	**For proof blocks**: include a `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` LaTeX comment **immediately before** the `\begin{proof}` environment (NOT inside it). Same verbatim rules — original language, original notation, every word. The informal proof body inside `\begin{proof}...\end{proof}` is the project's restated version in project notation, what the prover formalizes.
149	
150	When a source proof is too long for one verbatim block (multi-page construction): split the theorem into sub-lemmas in the directive's logical structure, and give each sub-statement its own `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`. One opaque mega-quote defeats verifiability. If even sub-splitting is impractical, report it in "Notes for Plan Agent" — do not silently drop the verbatim quote.
151	
152	**For Archon-original / project-bespoke results** (the directive does not name an external source for this block): the source lines are omitted — the block stands on the proof sketch alone.
153	
154	**The hard rule, explicit:**
155	
156	> **You may NEVER write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from training memory.** Every such line must be backed by a local file under `references/` that you have **opened and read in this session**, and the verbatim quote must be a character-by-character copy of text from that file.
157	
158	The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical in `% SOURCE:` is your discipline check. If, when reviewing your own draft, you find a block where you cannot point to the specific local file you read to produce the quote — you fabricated it. Delete the quote. Either dispatch a `reference-retriever` (see below) to obtain the missing source, or report the block as INCOMPLETE under "Notes for Plan Agent".
159	
160	If the directive named a source but the local reference file is missing or doesn't contain the specific statement you need:
161	
162	- **Dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** (see "Dispatching a reference-retriever" below) to fetch the missing material into `references/<slug>.md`.
163	- **Wait for the retriever to return**, then **open and read the new file**.
164	- THEN write the citation block, citing the new local file in the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
165	
166	If retrieval fails (paywall, broken link, no API key, not available online): mark the block `% SOURCE: <pointer> (verbatim text not yet retrieved)` and skip it. Report it as INCOMPLETE. Do NOT substitute a paraphrase, a recollection, or a translation as the verbatim quote — that's the iter-149 failure mode and the entire rule exists to prevent it.
167	
168	## Rules
169	
170	### What you CAN do
171	- Add new declaration blocks (definitions, lemmas, theorems, propositions, corollaries) under direction.
172	- Expand or revise existing prose / proof sketches in your assigned chapter.
173	- **Add, correct, or complete `\uses{...}` cross-references** so the dependency edges match the real mathematics. If your chapter's proof of `T` invokes lemma `L`, `T` must declare `\uses{lem:L}`; if a `\uses{}` points at a label that no longer exists, fix it. Verify with `leandag` (see "Verifying dependencies with leandag" below) — do not eyeball it.
174	- Adjust `\lean{...}` hints when the directive names a new Lean target.
175	- **Write entries for Lean helper declarations.** The project keeps a 1-to-1 Lean ↔ blueprint correspondence: when the directive lists a helper that exists only in Lean (a prover-created lemma, an instance, a decomposition step), give it a real entry — concise informal statement, `\label{}`, `\lean{}` with the exact Lean name, accurate `\uses{}`, and at least a one-line informal proof. "It's trivial" is not a reason to skip it: the entry is what carries the dependency edges and what helps a later prover fill the sorry. When the helper's Lean proof relies on a fact with no blueprint entry, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" (or create it too, if it belongs in your chapter).
176	- **Author a *Mathlib dependency anchor* and mark it `\mathlibok`** when your chapter relies on a result Mathlib already provides — write out the statement and point `\lean{}` at the real Mathlib declaration (see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below). This is the only marker you may add.
177	- **Remove a declaration block ONLY when the directive explicitly lists it for removal** (typically an isolated/orphaned statement the reviewer flagged `remove`). Default to wiring an isolated node into the graph (add the missing `\uses{}`), not deleting it — an isolated node is usually a missing edge, not dead weight. Never remove the project goal, and never remove a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
178	- Read `references/summary.md` and any reference that is in `references/` to ground your writing in the project's sources.
179	
180	### What you MUST do
181	- **Keep the chapter valid LaTeX.** Don't leave dangling `\begin{...}` without matching `\end{...}`. Compile-checking is the plan agent's responsibility but you must not introduce syntax errors.
182	- **Stay within your chapter.** Your declared write-domain is one `*.tex` file. The Archon CLI rejects writes outside it.
183	- **Define non-standard macros in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex`** before using them — but: that file is outside your write-domain, so you DON'T touch it. If the directive requires a new macro, you note in your report "needs macro `\foo`" and leave the LaTeX using the new command name; the plan agent adds the macro before next iter's typeset.
184	- **Use mathematical, not Lean-syntactic prose.** Describe the proof in the language of mathematics — definitions, set inclusions, ring maps, universal properties — not in Lean tactic syntax. The prover formalizes your math.
185	- **Follow citation discipline** for every block derived from external reference material (see "Citation discipline (the hard rule)" above). Each such block needs `% SOURCE:` with a local-file parenthetical, `% SOURCE QUOTE:` with a verbatim original-language quote, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` before the proof env when applicable, and a visible `\textit{Source: ...}` prefix. Never cite from memory — only from a local `references/<file>.md` you opened and read in this session.
186	- **Document every change** in your report, including which `references/<file>.md` files you opened (under "References consulted").
187	
188	### What you MUST NOT do
189	- **Do NOT reference project history.** No "since iteration N," "after our failed attempt," or session narrative. The blueprint must read as a standalone mathematical document.
190	- **Do NOT include Lean implementation details.** No tactic suggestions, typeclass wiring notes, or Lean code structure hints in the prose. If a Lean note is needed, it belongs in `.lean` files (the plan agent will use `lean-scaffolder` for that).
191	- **Do NOT add `\leanok` markers.** `\leanok` is earned by a sorry-free Lean proof and set by the deterministic `sync_leanok` phase — never by you. (`\mathlibok` is different: you MAY add it, but ONLY on a Mathlib dependency anchor — see "Mathlib dependency anchors" below — never on the project's own to-be-proved declarations.)
192	- **Do NOT edit other chapters.** Even when you spot a related issue, flag it in "Notes for Plan Agent" instead of fixing it.
193	- **Do NOT edit `content.tex`** (the top-level blueprint file that `\input`s the chapters).
194	- **Do NOT edit `.lean` files** or any other state file.
195	- **Do NOT write Lean syntax** — keep the chapter mathematical, not syntactic.
196	- **Do NOT write literal `REF` placeholders.** Never produce prose like "Definition~REF" or "Sections REF–REF". Cross-reference declarations with `\cref{<label>}` using the real label (look it up in the chapter or via `leandag`); when the target genuinely has no label yet, describe it by name in prose. The blueprint-doctor flags every literal `REF` as a malformed reference.
197	- **Do NOT interleave math delimiters.** Pick `\( … \)` for a formula and close it with `\)` — never produce `$ … \( … \) … $` or `X\(, hence \)Y` inversions; the doctor's `math-delim` lint flags every such site and the rendered output shreds mid-formula.
198	- **Do NOT paste bare label ids into prose.** "Thm.~th:main" is not a reference — write `\cref{...}` for project declarations, or the source's human-readable number ("Thm.~4.8") when quoting external material.
199	- **Do NOT use undefined macros.** Every `\foo` your prose needs must exist in `blueprint/src/macros/common.tex` or be `\providecommand`'d at the top of your chapter (chapter-local is fine; report a "needs macro" note for shared ones).
200	- **Do NOT touch protected blocks.** Your invocation prompt's "Protected by the mathematician" section lists `archon-protected.yaml` rules. A `statement`-protected label's declaration block (statement + `\label`/`\lean`/`\uses`) is frozen — you may still write its `\begin{proof}`; an `all`-protected label is entirely off-limits. You may freely `\uses{}` protected labels from other blocks. If your directive conflicts with a protection rule, stop and report the conflict instead of editing.
201	- **Do NOT expand scope.** Stick to what the directive listed under "Required content".
202	- **Do NOT fabricate citations.** Never write `% SOURCE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE:`, `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:`, or `\textit{Source: ...}` from memory. If you don't have a local `references/<file>.md` containing the source text, dispatch a retriever and wait. The `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical is the discipline check — if you cannot truthfully point to the file you read, the citation block is not allowed in the chapter.
203	- **Do NOT translate or restate the verbatim quote.** `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` contain the source's original language, original notation, every word as-is. Project-notation rewrites belong in the rendered prose body, not in the verbatim comment.
204	
205	## Mathlib dependency anchors (`\mathlibok`)
206	
207	When your chapter's proofs rely on a definition or lemma that **Mathlib already provides** (and the project will not re-prove), it is good practice — not just a bare `\uses{}` to nowhere — to make that dependency a first-class blueprint block: a **Mathlib dependency anchor**. Doing so:
208	
209	- makes the reliance explicit and readable (a mathematician sees exactly which Mathlib result the route stands on);
210	- gives `leandag` a real node, so the `\uses{}` resolves (no broken/unknown ref) and the node's effort is **done** rather than ∞;
211	- keeps it clear how much the route leans on Mathlib vs. on original work.
212	
213	Write the anchor like any statement block, with three differences:
214	
215	```latex
216	\begin{lemma}[Hensel's lemma]
217	  \label{lem:hensel_mathlib}
218	  \lean{Mathlib.RingTheory.Henselian.foo}   % the REAL Mathlib declaration
219	  \mathlibok
220	  \textit{Provided by Mathlib.}
221	  Statement of the result in the project's notation.
222	\end{lemma}
223	```
224	
225	- **`\lean{}` names the actual Mathlib declaration** (with its real namespace), so `leandag` matches the node to Mathlib.
226	- **`\mathlibok`** marks it as supplied by Mathlib — the DAG treats it as done; no `\begin{proof}` is needed.
227	- No `% SOURCE:`/`% SOURCE QUOTE:` block — Mathlib is the source; the `\lean{}` target *is* the citation.
228	
229	**The anti-hallucination rule (mirrors citation discipline):** mark `\mathlibok` ONLY when the statement genuinely exists in Mathlib and your stated form is faithful to it. A wrong or invented `\mathlibok` is worse than an ∞ hole — the loop will treat a non-existent result as available and skip proving a real gap. So:
230	
231	- Prefer anchors the **directive names** (the plan agent/reviewer identified them as Mathlib results).
232	- If you are not confident the exact statement is in Mathlib, do **not** mark `\mathlibok` — write the block without it (or leave the `\uses{}` for the reviewer to resolve) and flag it under "Notes for Plan Agent" so the reviewer can verify against Lean.
233	- Never put `\mathlibok` on one of the project's **own** to-be-proved declarations. That marker is for Mathlib-supplied results only; `\leanok` (project proofs) is never yours to add.
234	
235	## Reading references
236	
237	You write mathematics, not literature criticism — your prose must be grounded in authoritative sources, not your training memory. Before composing any new declaration block:
238	
239	1. **Read `references/summary.md`** to see the index of sources the project already has. Sources directly relevant to your chapter are your first stop.
240	2. **Read every reference file your directive names** under its `## References` section. The plan agent named those because the chapter needs them.
241	3. **Read anything in `references/summary.md` your directive didn't name but that is clearly relevant** to your chapter (same area, same theorem, same construction).
242	
243	Do NOT write content from your training memory when you could ground it in a reference instead. When the references say one thing and your memory says another, trust the references.
244	
245	## Dispatching a reference-retriever (when the project's sources don't have what you need)
246	
247	If, while drafting, you discover that the chapter needs material the project's `references/` doesn't contain, **dispatch a `reference-retriever` mid-session** rather than guessing or papering over the gap.
248	
249	Conditions for dispatch:
250	
251	- The directive named a source (`Smith 2018`, an arXiv ID) that isn't in `references/` yet.
252	- The required content is in a known textbook (Hartshorne, Vakil, Stacks Project, etc.) but the project hasn't summarized the relevant chapter.
253	- You need a Mathlib-adjacent source (nLab, math overflow, Stacks Project) the directive didn't anticipate.
254	
255	Dispatch (Bash; treat as blocking and await the report; in your write-domain only if it includes `references/**`):
256	
257	```
258	python3 .claude/tools/archon-subagent.py \
259	  --name reference-retriever \
260	  --slug <kebab-slug-for-the-source> \
261	  --directive-file .archon/logs/iter-NNN/<your-slug>/reference-retriever-<child-slug>-directive.md \
262	  --write-domain 'references/**'
263	```
264	
265	Write the directive file first; the directive format is documented in `.archon/subagents/reference-retriever.md`. The retriever returns when the new summary is on disk; **then resume writing**, citing the new reference.
266	
267	If your invocation's recorded write-domain does NOT include `references/**` (your `parent.write_domain` in `dispatch.jsonl`), the dispatch will be rejected. In that case, STOP writing the affected section, report the missing source in "Notes for Plan Agent", and finish the parts of the chapter you can still write. The plan agent will re-dispatch you next iter with the broader write-domain.
268	
269	## Workflow
270	
271	1. Read your directive completely.
272	2. **Read `references/summary.md`** and every reference your directive points at; also read sibling-chapter material that informs cross-references. Track which local files you actually opened — you will need their paths for the `% SOURCE:` parenthetical.
273	3. Read the target chapter currently on disk to see what's already there.
274	4. Plan the edits: which blocks to add, which to revise. Decide where each new block goes in the chapter's existing flow. For each block that derives from an external source, identify the specific local `references/<file>.md` containing the verbatim statement (and proof, if applicable). If no local file covers the statement, list it as "retrieval needed" for the next step.
275	5. **For every "retrieval needed" item, dispatch a `reference-retriever`** (see above) and **wait** for it to return. THEN open and read the newly-written `references/<slug>.md` before drafting the citing block. Do not draft citation blocks against pending or imagined sources.
276	6. Make the edits. For each citation block: copy `% SOURCE QUOTE:` and `% SOURCE QUOTE PROOF:` content character-by-character from the local reference file — original language, original notation, every word. Do not transcribe from a window of the file you "remember reading"; have the file open and copy.
277	7. Verify the file is still valid LaTeX at a glance (no unmatched begin/end, balanced braces in `\label`/`\uses`/`\lean`). Spot-check that every `% SOURCE:` line has a non-empty `(read from references/<file>.md)` parenthetical and that the named file exists.
278	8. **Verify dependencies with `leandag`** (see the section below): confirm your chapter introduced no broken `\uses{}` and left none of your blocks unintentionally isolated. Fix the edges and re-check.
279	9. Write your report. List under "References consulted" every local file you opened in step 2 and step 5.
280	
281	## Verifying dependencies with leandag
282	
283	`leandag` parses the real Lean ↔ blueprint dependency DAG, so use it to check your `\uses{}` instead of trusting your memory of the graph. It is read-only on the blueprint (it never edits your chapter); run it after you make edits:
284	
285	```
286	leandag build --json                       # report: unknown_uses, unmatched_lean, isolated count
287	leandag query --isolated --chapter <c>     # isolated nodes in your chapter (no edges in or out)
288	leandag show isolated                       # all isolated nodes, project-wide
289	```
290	
291	`leandag` is on the same PATH as `archon`; it is the one tool you use to query the DAG.
292	
293	What to do with the output, scoped to **your chapter**:
294	
295	- **`unknown_uses` naming a label in your chapter** → you wrote a `\uses{}` pointing at a label that doesn't exist. Fix the label, or — if the target genuinely belongs in your chapter and the directive calls for it — add the missing declaration. If the missing label lives in another chapter, leave a correct `\uses{}` to it and note it under "Notes for Plan Agent" (you don't edit that chapter).
296	- **A block you wrote shows up as isolated** → it has no `\uses{}` out and nothing uses it. This is almost always a *missing edge*: add the `\uses{}` that ties it to the declarations its statement/proof actually depends on (and check whether a sibling result should `\uses{}` it). Only treat an isolated block as removable when your directive explicitly authorized removing it.
297	- **Removal** (directive-authorized only): delete the orphaned block cleanly and record it in your report. Never remove the goal or a block whose `\lean{}` still names a live Lean declaration.
298	
299	Keep `leandag` edits inside your assigned chapter — fixing a dependency that lives in another chapter is a "Notes for Plan Agent" item, not a cross-chapter edit.
300

321	  - Proof sketch added: <Y/N + brief shape>

The plan agent and the reviewer use this list to verify that every `% SOURCE:` parenthetical points at a file you actually read. Omit this section only when you wrote zero citation blocks (Archon-original chapter).>

1	\chapter{Flat base change for the pushforward of a quasi-coherent sheaf ($i=0$)}
2	\label{chap:Cohomology_FlatBaseChange}
3	% archon:covers AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChange.lean AlgebraicJacobian/Cohomology/FlatBaseChangeGlobal.lean
4	% SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
5	% base change, I", and Tag 02KH (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex and
6	% references/stacks-coherent.md).
7	
8	\section{Setup and motivation}
9	
10	This chapter establishes the $i=0$ (i.e.\ \(H^0\) / direct-image) case of flat
11	base change: the formation of the pushforward \(f_*\mathcal{F}\) of a
12	quasi-coherent sheaf commutes with flat base change on the target. It is the
13	foundational, fully self-contained slice of the cohomology-and-base-change
14	package that ultimately underlies relative representability statements; the
15	higher derived cases \(R^i f_*\) for \(i \geq 1\) are treated separately and are
16	out of scope here.
17	
18	Throughout, \(f : X \to S\) is a morphism of schemes that is quasi-compact and
19	quasi-separated, and \(\mathcal{F}\) is a quasi-coherent \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module.
20	A base change of \(f\) along an arbitrary morphism \(g : S' \to S\) is recorded
21	by the cartesian square obtained from the fibre product
22	\(X' = X \times_S S'\):
23	\[
24	  \begin{array}{ccc}
25	    X' & \xrightarrow{\;g'\;} & X \\[2pt]
26	    {\scriptstyle f'}\big\downarrow & & \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle f} \\[2pt]
27	    S' & \xrightarrow{\;g\;} & S
28	  \end{array}
29	\]
30	Here \(f' : X' \to S'\) is the base change of \(f\), and \(g' : X' \to X\) is the
31	first projection. We write \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\) for the pullback
32	of \(\mathcal{F}\) to \(X'\).
33	
34	\section{The canonical base-change map}
35	
36	\begin{definition}\leanok
37	[Base-change map for the pushforward]
38	  \label{def:pushforward_base_change_map}
39	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforwardBaseChangeMap}
40	  % SOURCE: [Stacks Project], Cohomology of Schemes, Section "Cohomology and
41	  % base change, I", base-change diagram (\ref{equation-base-change-diagram})
42	  % (read from references/stacks-coherent.tex, L877--904).
43	  % SOURCE QUOTE: "Let $f : X \to S$ be a morphism of schemes.
44	  % Let $\mathcal{F}$ be a quasi-coherent sheaf on $X$.
45	  % Suppose further that $g : S' \to S$ is any morphism of schemes. Denote
46	  % $X' = X_{S'} = S' \times_S X$ the base change of $X$ and denote
47	  % $f' : X' \to S'$ the base change of $f$.
48	  % Also write $g' : X' \to X$ the projection,
49	  % and set $\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}$."
50	  \textit{Source: Stacks Project, Cohomology of Schemes, \S\,Cohomology and base
51	  change, I.}
52	  Let \(f : X \to S\) be a morphism of schemes, \(\mathcal{F}\) a quasi-coherent
53	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-module, and \(g : S' \to S\) an arbitrary morphism, giving the
54	  cartesian square above with projection \(g' : X' \to X\) and
55	  \(\mathcal{F}' = (g')^*\mathcal{F}\). The \emph{base-change map for the
56	  pushforward} is the canonical morphism of \(\mathcal{O}_{S'}\)-modules
57	  \[
58	    g^*\bigl(f_*\mathcal{F}\bigr) \longrightarrow f'_*\bigl((g')^*\mathcal{F}\bigr)
59	    = f'_*\mathcal{F}' .
60	  \]
61	  It is adjoint to the morphism
62	  \(f_*\mathcal{F} \to g_*\, f'_*\mathcal{F}' = f_*\,(g')_*\mathcal{F}'\) obtained
63	  by applying \(f_*\) to the unit
64	  \(\mathcal{F} \to (g')_*(g')^*\mathcal{F}\) of the
65	  \(((g')^*, (g')_*)\)-adjunction, using the commutativity
66	  \(g \circ f' = f \circ g'\) of the square.
67	\end{definition}
68	
69	\section{The affine case}
70	
71	\subsection{Locality of isomorphisms for quasi-coherent sheaf morphisms}
72	
73	Before treating the affine computation we record three project-local supplements
74	to Mathlib. Mathlib provides the per-open criterion
75	(\(\mathrm{IsIso}\,\varphi \iff \forall U,\ \mathrm{IsIso}(\varphi_U)\)) and a
76	stalkwise isomorphism criterion for \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-valued morphisms, but
77	it does not package the stalk-local or basis-local criterion at the level of
78	morphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. The following lemmas bridge that gap; they
79	are exactly the locality tools used in the first reduction of the affine
80	base-change lemma below, where one checks the base-change map after restricting to
81	the affine opens of the base. As bespoke infrastructure they carry no external
82	citation and stand on their proof sketches.
83	
84	\begin{lemma}\leanok
85	[Stalk-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
86	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
87	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_stalkFunctor_map}
88	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
89	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if the
90	  underlying morphism of abelian-group presheaves induces an isomorphism on the
91	  stalk at every point \(x \in X\).
92	\end{lemma}
93	
94	\begin{proof}
95	
96	
97	
98	  If \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism, then so is its image under the forgetful
99	  functor to abelian-group presheaves, and the stalk functor at each \(x\),
100	  being a functor, carries isomorphisms to isomorphisms. Conversely, assume the
101	  stalk map is an isomorphism at every \(x\). Package the underlying abelian-group
102	  presheaves of \(M\) and \(N\) as sheaves of abelian groups, and \(\varphi\) as a
103	  morphism of these sheaves; the stalkwise-isomorphism hypothesis lets us apply the
104	  \(\mathrm{TopCat.Sheaf}\)-level stalk-local isomorphism criterion to conclude that
105	  this morphism of sheaves of abelian groups is an isomorphism. Pushing it through
106	  the forgetful functor to presheaves yields an isomorphism of the underlying
107	  abelian-group presheaf morphisms, and since the forgetful functor from
108	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules to abelian-group presheaves reflects isomorphisms,
109	  \(\varphi\) itself is an isomorphism.
110	\end{proof}
111	
112	\begin{lemma}\leanok
113	[Basis-local criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
114	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
115	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_of_isIso_app_of_isBasis}
116	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
117	  Let \(X\) be a scheme, let \(\{B_i\}_{i \in \iota}\) be a family of opens whose
118	  range is a basis of the topology of \(X\), and let \(\varphi : M \to N\) be a
119	  morphism of sheaves of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. If \(\varphi\) restricts to an
120	  isomorphism on the sections over every basic open \(B_i\), then \(\varphi\) is an
121	  isomorphism.
122	\end{lemma}
123	
124	\begin{proof}
125	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk}
126	  By Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_iff_stalk} it suffices to prove that the induced
127	  stalk map is bijective at each point \(x \in X\). For injectivity, a germ that
128	  vanishes at \(x\) already vanishes on some open neighbourhood, which may be
129	  shrunk to a basic open \(B_i\); on \(B_i\) the section map \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is
130	  injective, so the original section is zero, giving injectivity of the stalk map
131	  from injectivity over the basis. For surjectivity, an arbitrary germ at \(x\) is
132	  represented by a section of \(N\) over some basic open \(B_i\) containing \(x\);
133	  since \(\varphi_{B_i}\) is surjective, this section lifts to a section of \(M\)
134	  over \(B_i\), whose germ at \(x\) maps to the given germ. Thus the stalk map is
135	  bijective, and \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism.
136	\end{proof}
137	
138	\begin{lemma}\leanok
139	[Affine-open locality criterion for isomorphisms of \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules]
140	  \label{lem:modules_isIso_iff_affineOpens}
141	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens}
142	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
143	  Let \(X\) be a scheme and \(\varphi : M \to N\) a morphism of sheaves of
144	  \(\mathcal{O}_X\)-modules. Then \(\varphi\) is an isomorphism if and only if it
145	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of \(X\).
146	\end{lemma}
147	
148	\begin{proof}
149	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis}
150	  The forward implication is immediate, since the section functor over a fixed open
151	  preserves isomorphisms. For the converse, the affine opens of a scheme form a
152	  basis of its topology, so the claim is the special case of
153	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} in which the basis is the family of
154	  affine opens.
155	\end{proof}
156	
157	\subsection{The affine computation}
158	
159	The whole result rests on the following elementary computation in the affine
160	setting, where the base-change map is visibly an isomorphism because tensoring is
161	associative. Before stating it we isolate the one ingredient not yet available as
162	a ready-made result on which the affine reduction turns: the description of the
163	pushforward of a tilde-module along a morphism of affine schemes.
164	
165	We first record three bespoke \(\Gamma\)-fragment supports that compute the global
166	sections of an affine pushforward; they carry no external citation and stand on
167	their proof sketches.
168	
169	\begin{lemma}
170	
171	\leanok
172	[Naturality of the global-sections comparison square]
173	  \label{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
174	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
175	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, and write
176	  \(\mathrm{gs}_R : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R, \top) \cong R\) and
177	  \(\mathrm{gs}_{R'} : \Gamma(\operatorname{Spec} R', \top) \cong R'\) for the
178	  global-sections isomorphisms of the structure sheaves. Then the ring square
179	  \[
180	    \mathrm{gs}_R \;\circ\; (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}
181	    \;=\; \varphi \;\circ\; \mathrm{gs}_{R'}
182	  \]
183	  commutes, where \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{\sharp}_{\top}\) is the
184	  top-open component of the structure-sheaf comparison map underlying
185	  \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\).
186	\end{lemma}
187	
188	\begin{proof}
189	
190	
191	
192	  This is the naturality of the unit/counit of the
193	  \(\Gamma \dashv \operatorname{Spec}\) adjunction read off at the top open: the
194	  global-sections map of \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, under the
195	  global-sections isomorphisms on each side, conjugate to \(\varphi\) itself.
196	  Transposing the naturality square of the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) unit gives
197	  exactly the asserted ring equation.
198	\end{proof}
199	
200	\begin{lemma}
201	
202	\leanok
203	[Global sections of an affine pushforward]
204	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
205	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIso}
206	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
207	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let
208	  \(N\) be an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module. Then there is a canonical
209	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
210	  \[
211	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\bigr)
212	    \;\cong\;
213	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma N\bigr),
214	  \]
215	  identifying the \(R\)-module of global sections of the pushforward with the
216	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\) of the \(R'\)-module of global sections
217	  of \(N\).
218	\end{lemma}
219	
220	\begin{proof}
221	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
222	  Because \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(\top) = \top\), the global sections of
223	  the pushforward \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\) are, on underlying abelian
224	  groups, the global sections of \(N\) with no transport along the open-set
225	  identification. The two sides therefore carry the same underlying abelian group;
226	  both unwind to nested restriction-of-scalars towers along the structure-sheaf
227	  global-sections maps. These towers are reconciled by applying the
228	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the ring
229	  equation of
230	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}, which shows the
231	  resulting \(R\)-action is exactly restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\).
232	\end{proof}
233	
234	\begin{lemma}
235	
236	\leanok
237	[Global sections of an affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
238	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
239	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardTildeIso}
240	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
241	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
242	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
243	  \[
244	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \widetilde{M}\bigr)
245	    \;\cong\;
246	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M .
247	  \]
248	\end{lemma}
249	
250	\begin{proof}
251	  \uses{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}
252	  Specialise Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} to \(N = \widetilde{M}\) and
253	  compose with the tilde-unit isomorphism
254	  \(\Gamma(\widetilde{M}) \cong M\) of \(R'\)-modules (which restricts to an
255	  isomorphism of \(R\)-modules after applying \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi\)).
256	\end{proof}
257	
258	\begin{lemma}
259	
260	\leanok
261	[Sections of an affine pushforward over an arbitrary open]
262	  \label{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
263	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.gammaPushforwardIsoAt}
264	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
265	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings, let \(N\) be
266	  an \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\)-module, and let \(U\) be an open of
267	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\), with preimage
268	  \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}(U)\) in \(\operatorname{Spec}(R')\). Then
269	  there is a canonical isomorphism of \(R\)-modules
270	  \[
271	    \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)
272	    \;\cong\;
273	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, V)\bigr) .
274	  \]
275	  This is the open-indexed generalization of
276	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}, which is the case \(U = \top\) (so that
277	  \(V = \top\) as well). The family \(\{e_U\}_U\) of these isomorphisms is moreover
278	  natural in the open argument: the structure-sheaf restriction maps on the two
279	  sides commute with the isomorphisms, so the family is a natural isomorphism of the
280	  two presheaves of \(R\)-modules in the open variable (this naturality is
281	  established in the proof below). For \(U = D(a)\) it is exactly the linear
282	  equivalence \(e_{D(a)}\) of movement~(1) in the proof of
283	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.

286	\begin{proof}
287	  \uses{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}
288	  The construction copies that of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso} verbatim,
289	  with the top open \(\top\) replaced throughout by the arbitrary open \(U\) (and
290	  its preimage \(V\)). The point that makes this replacement free is that the
291	  \(R\)-module structure on the sections is supplied \emph{uniformly} by
292	  restriction of scalars along the global-sections ring map at the top open,
293	  independently of the evaluation open; consequently both sides unwind to the same
294	  nested restriction-of-scalars towers, reconciled by applying the
295	  restriction-of-scalars composition identity twice and substituting the
296	  \(\top\)-level ring equation of
297	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}. Naturality in the open
298	  follows because every constituent of the construction is either a structure-sheaf
299	  restriction map or conjugation by a fixed ring map, and all of these commute with
300	  further restriction along an inclusion of opens.
301	
302	  \medskip
303	  \emph{Naturality of the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) in the open (remark).} The family is
304	  in fact a natural isomorphism between two presheaves of \(R\)-modules on
305	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\): the source presheaf
306	  \(U \mapsto \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N,\, U\bigr)\), with the
307	  structure-sheaf restriction maps of the pushforward, and the target presheaf
308	  \(U \mapsto
309	    \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(N,\, (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U)\bigr)\),
310	  with the structure-sheaf restriction maps of \(N\) transported through the preimage
311	  operation \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) and read as \(R\)-linear maps by
312	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). Concretely, for any inclusion of opens
313	  \(U' \subseteq U\), with \(V = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U\) and
314	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U'\), the square with horizontal maps
315	  \(e_U, e_{U'}\) and vertical maps the respective structure-sheaf restrictions
316	  commutes. The reason is structural: each \(e_U\) is a composite whose constituents
317	  are of exactly two kinds — structure-sheaf restriction maps (of \(N\),
318	  respectively of \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* N\)) and identity-on-carrier
319	  \(\operatorname{restrictScalars}\) repackagings (conjugation by the \emph{fixed},
320	  open-independent ring map supplied by the restriction-of-scalars reconciliation and
321	  by the \(\top\)-level ring equation of
322	  Lemma~\ref{lem:globalSectionsIso_hom_comp_specMap_appTop}). Restriction maps commute
323	  with further restriction by the presheaf functoriality axiom (using
324	  \(V' = (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U' \subseteq
325	  (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}U = V\), as \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}\) is
326	  monotone), and conjugation by a fixed ring map commutes with restriction because the
327	  ring map does not depend on \(U\); the naturality square is the paste of these
328	  component squares, and each \(e_U\) being an isomorphism makes the family a natural
329	  isomorphism. This open-naturality is exactly the compatibility invoked, at the
330	  inclusion \(D(a) \subseteq \top\), in the proof of
331	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}.

472	\begin{lemma}\leanok
473	[Affine pushforward of a tilde-module]
474	  \label{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
475	  \lean{AlgebraicGeometry.pushforward_spec_tilde_iso}
476	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso,
477	        lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars,
478	        lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt,
479	        lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
480	% NOTE: the carrier-wall obstruction to `hloc` is resolved (restriction-of-scalars /
481	% scalar-tower instances feed lem:powers_restrictScalars; lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule
482	% supplies the R'-side localization). The single remaining obligation is the open-naturality of
483	% the section comparison e_{D(a)}, established as a prose remark in the proof of
484	% lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt; the proof sketch below derives each per-a hloc(a) from the top-level
485	% localization plus that naturality square for the inclusion D(a) ⊆ ⊤, so no section-level square
486	% is hand-proved per a.
487	  Let \(\varphi : R \to R'\) be a homomorphism of commutative rings and let \(M\)
488	  be an \(R'\)-module. Then there is a canonical isomorphism of
489	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
490	  \[
491	    \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\,\widetilde{M}
492	    \;\cong\;
493	    \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)},
494	  \]
495	  where \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi : \operatorname{Spec}(R') \to
496	  \operatorname{Spec}(R)\) is the induced morphism of affine schemes,
497	  \(\widetilde{(-)}\) denotes the quasi-coherent sheaf associated to a module, and
498	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) is the \(R\)-module obtained from \(M\) by
499	  restriction of scalars along \(\varphi\). That is, pushing the quasi-coherent
500	  sheaf \(\widetilde{M}\) forward along \(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\) is, up to
501	  this canonical identification, the tilde of the restriction of scalars of \(M\).
502	
503	  This is the classical fact that the direct image of a quasi-coherent sheaf along
504	  a morphism of affine schemes is computed on modules by restriction of scalars; it
505	  is the affine companion of the pullback formula (the affine case of the
506	  pushforward analogue of Stacks ``$\widetilde{M}$ and pullback''). It is recorded
507	  here as bespoke project infrastructure because the affine-pushforward
508	  identification does not appear as a ready-made result in the existing
509	  quasi-coherent sheaf theory.
510	\end{lemma}
511	
512	\begin{proof}
513	  \uses{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis, lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso, lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional, lem:powers_restrictScalars, lem:gammaPushforwardIso, lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt, lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}
514	  We build the isomorphism \emph{directly on a basis of basic opens}. This is the
515	  non-circular route: checking the comparison map on basic opens yields the object
516	  isomorphism, and quasi-coherence of the pushforward is then a corollary, not a
517	  prerequisite. (One must not reverse this order: the global-sections comparison
518	  \(\Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde{M}\bigr) \cong
519	  \operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) of
520	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso} alone would only upgrade to an object
521	  isomorphism once the pushforward is already known to be quasi-coherent, so
522	  quasi-coherence cannot be deduced from an object isomorphism built that way.)
523	
524	  \emph{The comparison morphism.} There is a canonical morphism of
525	  \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\)-modules
526	  \[
527	    \alpha : \widetilde{\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)}
528	    \longrightarrow \bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*\widetilde{M},
529	  \]
530	  namely the tilde-adjoint of the global-sections identification of
531	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardTildeIso}. We show \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism.
532	
533	  \medskip
534	  \emph{Reduction to basic opens.} The basic opens \(D(a)\), for \(a \in R\), form a
535	  basis of the topology of \(\operatorname{Spec}(R)\). By the basis-local criterion
536	  Lemma~\ref{lem:modules_isIso_of_isBasis} it suffices to check that \(\alpha\)
537	  restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over each basic open \(D(a)\).
538	
539	  \medskip
540	  \emph{The computation over \(D(a)\).} On the source, the sections of
541	  \(\widetilde{(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M)}\) over \(D(a)\) are the
542	  localization \(\bigl(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\bigr)[1/a]\) as an
543	  \(R[1/a]\)-module. On the target, since \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) =
544	  D(\varphi a)\), the sections of \(\bigl(\operatorname{Spec}\varphi\bigr)_*
545	  \widetilde{M}\) over \(D(a)\) are the sections of \(\widetilde{M}\) over
546	  \(D(\varphi a)\), i.e.\ the localization \(M[1/\varphi a]\), regarded as an
547	  \(R[1/a]\)-module by restriction of scalars along the induced map
548	  \(R[1/a] \to R'[1/\varphi a]\). These two localizations agree: the element \(a\)
549	  acts on \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) through \(\varphi a\), so localizing
550	  \(\operatorname{restr}_\varphi M\) at the multiplicative set generated by \(a\) is
551	  the same module as localizing \(M\) at the multiplicative set generated by
552	  \(\varphi a\). Both are the universal localization of
553	  \(M\) inverting \(\varphi a\), so the comparison map \(\alpha\) is an isomorphism
554	  on the sections over \(D(a)\). As this holds for every basic open, \(\alpha\) is
555	  an isomorphism.
556	
557	  \medskip
558	  \emph{The explicit formalization route over \(D(a)\).} The agreement of the two
559	  localizations just described is the entire content, but it must be realized
560	  through the global ring \(R\), because the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
561	  sections over \(D(a)\) is built from the global-sections module of
562	  \(N := (\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M\) rather than directly from
563	  \(R[1/a]\). The reduction therefore proceeds exactly as the conditional
564	  assembly Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} prescribes: it
565	  suffices to supply, for each \(a \in R\), the hypothesis \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) that
566	  the structure-sheaf restriction \(\Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) exhibits
567	  \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) as the localization of \(\Gamma(N, \top)\) at
568	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\). The \(R\)-action on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) is the honest
569	  restriction-of-scalars action carried by the scalar tower \(R \to R' \to {-}\)
570	  determined by \(\varphi\); against that structure the ring-change converse
571	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applicable, so the carrier of the
572	  \(R\)-module is exactly the one to which the localization claim refers, and no
573	  recursively-defined or ad-hoc section-level scalar action intervenes.
574	  We obtain \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transporting the already-known \(R'\)-side
575	  localization (Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}), in
576	  element-free fashion, across the open-indexed section comparison of
577	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} \emph{using its naturality in the open}
578	  (the naturality remark in the proof of
579	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}). This proceeds in three
580	  movements.
581	
582	  \emph{(1) The \(D(a)\)-level linear equivalence.} This is the component at
583	  \(U = D(a)\) of the open-indexed family of
584	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the \(R\)-linear isomorphism
585	  \[
586	    e_{D(a)} : \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
587	      \;\cong\;
588	      \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr) ,
589	  \]
590	  using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
591	  definitional. No fresh \(D(a)\)-level construction is needed: this is precisely the
592	  open-indexed isomorphism already supplied by
593	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} evaluated at the basic open \(D(a)\).
594	
595	  \emph{(2) The naturality square for \(D(a) \subseteq \top\).} The compatibility
596	  that relates \(e_{D(a)}\) to the \(\top\)-level isomorphism
597	  \(e_\top\) (the global-sections comparison of
598	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIso}) is precisely the open-naturality square of the
599	  family \(\{e_U\}_U\) (established in the proof of
600	  Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}), instantiated at the inclusion
601	  \(D(a) \subseteq \top\): the square
602	  \[
603	    \begin{array}{ccc}
604	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, \top\bigr)
605	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_\top\;}
606	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, \top)\bigr) \\[4pt]
607	      {\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}}\big\downarrow & &
608	        \big\downarrow{\scriptstyle \mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}} \\[4pt]
609	      \Gamma\bigl((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_*\widetilde M,\, D(a)\bigr)
610	        & \xrightarrow{\;e_{D(a)}\;}
611	        & \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\bigl(\Gamma(\widetilde M,\, D(\varphi a))\bigr)
612	    \end{array}
613	  \]
614	  commutes, using \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)^{-1}D(a) = D(\varphi a)\), which is
615	  definitional. Because the family \(\{e_U\}_U\) is a natural isomorphism, this single
616	  square is available uniformly in \(a\); there is no per-\(a\) section-level identity
617	  to re-prove by hand. It intertwines the source restriction
618	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) on the pushforward with the (restriction-of-scalars
619	  reading of the) target restriction \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)}\) on
620	  \(\widetilde M\).
621	
622	  \emph{(3) Discharging \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) by transport.} The target restriction
623	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(\varphi a)} : \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top) \to
624	  \Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a))\) exhibits \(\Gamma(\widetilde M, D(\varphi a)) =
625	  M[1/\varphi a]\) as the \(\mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)-localization of
626	  \(M = \Gamma(\widetilde M, \top)\) over \(R'\); this is exactly
627	  Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule}. Apply the ring-change converse
628	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} (with \(R\)-algebra \(A = R'\) and submonoid
629	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\), so that
630	  \(\operatorname{algMap}_{R'}(\mathrm{powers}(a)) = \mathrm{powers}(\varphi a)\)) —
631	  its \(\operatorname{Algebra} R\, R'\) and scalar-tower hypotheses being supplied by
632	  the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\) — to read this same map as
633	  the \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization over \(R\). The commuting naturality square
634	  of movement (2) intertwines this target restriction with the source restriction
635	  \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)} : \Gamma(N, \top) \to \Gamma(N, D(a))\) by way of the
636	  isomorphisms \(e_\top\) and \(e_{D(a)}\); transporting the localization property
637	  across that square — via the transport-along-an-equivalence principle for localized
638	  modules — yields that \(\mathrm{res}_{\top \to D(a)}\) is the
639	  \(\mathrm{powers}(a)\)-localization on \(\Gamma(N, D(a))\) over \(R\), i.e.\
640	  \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\). No section-level computation specific to the individual \(a\)
641	  is performed: the only \(a\)-dependent input is the instantiation of the single
642	  natural-isomorphism square at \(D(a)\). Feeding the family
643	  \(\{\mathrm{hloc}(a)\}_{a \in R}\) to the conditional assembly
644	  Lemma~\ref{lem:pushforward_spec_tilde_iso_conditional} produces the unconditional
645	  object isomorphism \(\alpha\).
646	
647	  \medskip
648	  \emph{Naturality in the open drives the transport.} The comparison assembled
649	  here, \((\operatorname{Spec}\varphi)_* \circ \widetilde{(-)} \cong
650	  \widetilde{(-)} \circ \operatorname{restr}_\varphi\), is natural in \emph{both}
651	  arguments — in the module \(M\) and in the open \(U\). The naturality in the
652	  module is the usual functoriality; the naturality in the open is the naturality
653	  remark in the proof of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt}: the family
654	  \(\{e_U\}_U\) of Lemma~\ref{lem:gammaPushforwardIsoAt} commutes with the
655	  structure-sheaf restriction maps on both sides. It is precisely this
656	  open-naturality that drives the localization transport \emph{uniformly}: the
657	  per-\(a\) localization fact \(\mathrm{hloc}(a)\) follows from the \(\top\)-level
658	  localization Lemma~\ref{lem:tildeRestriction_isLocalizedModule} together with that
659	  naturality square for the inclusion
660	  \(D(a) \hookrightarrow \top\), rather than from a section-level naturality square
661	  re-proved by hand for each individual \(a\). This is the single remaining
662	  obligation of the construction; the carrier of the \(R\)-action on the pushforward
663	  sections is the restriction-of-scalars structure along \(\varphi\), against which
664	  Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} applies directly.
665	
666	  The \(R\)-module structure on the \(R'\)-side sections to which the ring-change
667	  converse Lemma~\ref{lem:powers_restrictScalars} is applied is the honest

3211	This is the active route for the section-level identity (obligation~2). It replaces the abandoned
3212	conjugate-side / adjoint-mate discharge (now flagged superseded above). The idea is elementary: in the
3213	fully-affine model the global-sections functor on quasi-coherent
3214	\((\operatorname{Spec} R')\)-modules is conservative — every object is the \(\operatorname{Spec}\) of a

3216	isomorphism precisely when it is bijective on underlying modules. We exhibit a two-sided inverse for
3217	the section-level base-change map and verify the two round-trips on pure tensors by an element chase,
3218	so the conjugate (mate) normal form is never produced and the \(X.\mathrm{Modules}\) instance diamond
3219	is never crossed.

theorem pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange
    {R R' A : CommRingCat.{u}} (ψ : R ⟶ R') (φ : R ⟶ A) (M : ModuleCat.{u} A) :
    IsIso ((moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R')).map
      (pushforwardBaseChangeMap (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ)
        (Limits.pullback.snd (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ))
        (Limits.pullback.fst (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ))
        (IsPullback.of_hasPullback (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ)).w (tilde M))) := by
  -- Assemble the chain (blueprint `lem:pushforward_base_change_mate_cancelBaseChange`): the domain
  -- read `Θ_src` (`base_change_mate_domain_read`) and codomain read `Θ_tgt`
  -- (`base_change_mate_codomain_read`, resting on the `pullbackSpecIso` leg identification
  -- `pullback_fst_snd_specMap_tensor`) identify `Γ(α)`'s domain/codomain with `R' ⊗_R M` and
  -- `(A ⊗_R R') ⊗_A M`; the generator trace `base_change_mate_generator_trace` shows the conjugate
  -- `Θ_src⁻¹ ≫ Γ(α) ≫ Θ_tgt` is an isomorphism (it is `cancelBaseChange⁻¹`, no flatness).
  -- Conjugating back, `Γ(α)` is itself an isomorphism.
  haveI hconj := base_change_mate_generator_trace ψ φ M
  set D := base_change_mate_domain_read ψ φ M with hD
  set C := base_change_mate_codomain_read ψ φ M with hC
  set Γα := (moduleSpecΓFunctor (R := R')).map
    (pushforwardBaseChangeMap (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ)
      (Limits.pullback.snd (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ))
      (Limits.pullback.fst (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ))
      (IsPullback.of_hasPullback (Spec.map φ) (Spec.map ψ)).w (tilde M)) with hΓα
  have heq : Γα = D.hom ≫ (D.inv ≫ Γα ≫ C.hom) ≫ C.inv := by
    simp [Category.assoc]
  rw [heq]
  infer_instance

/-- **Affine-local compatibility of the base-change map (locality reduction).** For `f` affine
and `F` quasi-coherent, the base-change map `pushforwardBaseChangeMap` is an isomorphism as soon
as it restricts to an isomorphism on the sections over every affine open of the base `S'`. This is
the locality reduction underlying `affineBaseChange_pushforward_iso`: it discharges the
affine-open locality criterion `Modules.isIso_iff_isIso_app_affineOpens` for the base-change map,
the per-affine-open hypothesis being the affine–affine section assertion supplied (over each chart)
by the section-level computation. The `[IsAffineHom f]`/`[F.IsQuasicoherent]` hypotheses are
carried because the per-open assertion `H` is only available under them (they make `X` restrict to
`Spec A` and `F` to `M^~` over each affine chart); the reduction itself is the locality criterion.
See blueprint `lem:base_change_map_affine_local`. -/
